revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:蛇行剣]][[zh:蛇行劍]]


{{Multiple issues|source=February 2021|footnotes=February 2021|qq=}}
[[File:茶すり山古墳出土 素環頭大刀・鉄刀・蛇行剣.JPG|thumb|A meandering sword (rear left) excavated from {{ill|Chasuriyama Kofun|en|Chasuriyama Kofun|qid=Q103903111|lt=Chasuriyama Kofun|1=Chasuriyama Kofun}}, {{ill|Asago, Hyōgo|en|Asago, Hyōgo|qid=Q720402|lt=Asago City|1=Asago, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, along with its restoration (rear right).]]
[[File:富雄丸山古墳出土 蛇行剣.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Meandering sword excavated from {{ill|Tomiomaru-yama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomiomaru-yama Kofun|1=Tomio-Maruyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28688548}}|qq=}}{{small|Photographed during an exhibition at the {{ill|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|en|The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture|qid=Q112961122|lt=Kashihara Archaeological Institute|1=The Museum, Archaeological Institute of Kashihara, Nara Prefecture}}.|qq=}}]]
The '''meandering sword''' (蛇行剣, Dakōken) is a type of {{ill|Iron|en|Iron|qid=Q677|lt=iron|1=Iron}} sword from Japan's {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun Period|1=Kofun period}}. Depending on its size, it is sometimes classified as a halberd. As its name suggests, its blade curves and undulates like a snake in motion, which is the origin of its name.<ref>Named "Meandering Curved Blade Sword" by {{ill|Morikazu Gotō|ja|後藤守一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Morikazu Gotō|1=Shūichi Gotō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488456}}. See reference: Masafumi Itō, *Meandering Swords Unearthed in the Chūbu Region*.</ref>
== Overview ==
The meandering sword is an iron weapon primarily found in western Japan. Based on its shape and the limited number of discoveries, it is believed to have been used for rituals rather than as a practical weapon.<ref>According to Shigeru Tanaka’s research, "The meandering sword was forged for ritualistic purposes rather than as a practical tool and was likely kept by individuals in priestly positions." Reference: Masafumi Itō, *Meandering Swords Unearthed in the Chūbu Region*.</ref> These swords have been excavated from {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} (tumuli) and underground horizontal tombs. Originating in the southern Kyushu region ({{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|qid=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}}), the meandering sword spread to the {{ill|Kinai Region|ja|近畿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinai Region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近畿地方|1=Kansai region|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q164256}} by the early {{ill|5th century|en|5th century|qid=Q8095|lt=5th century|1=5th century}} (see {{section|Notes|qq=}}). Its unique design makes it an important archaeological artifact that reflects Japan's distinct weaponry during the Kofun Period.
The earliest known discovery of a meandering sword occurred in {{ill|1925|en|1925|qid=Q18107|lt=Taisho 13|1=1925}} at the Jōdōji-yama Kofun (also known as Minamikata No. 39 Mound) in {{ill|Nobeoka|en|Nobeoka|qid=Q653389|lt=Hyūga|1=Nobeoka}}, where it was identified by {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|qid=Q707532|lt=Ryūzō Torii|1=Torii Ryūzō}}. Torii noted its resemblance to the {{ill|Kris|en|Kris|qid=Q331220|lt=kris|1=Kris}} of Indonesia, sparking interest in potential connections to Southeast Asia's snake-related symbolism.<ref>The kris remains a living cultural symbol, while the meandering sword’s magical significance was forgotten after the 4th-6th centuries, except for fragments in ancient records like the *Fudoki*. By comparison, the meandering sword is significantly older than related designs such as the Chinese {{ill|Serpentine Spear|ja|蛇矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Serpentine Spear|1=Serpent spear|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6131024}} and European {{ill|Flamberge|en|Flamberge|qid=Q1328777|lt=flamberge|1=Flamberge}}.</ref>
In {{ill|1986|en|1986|qid=Q2430|lt=Showa 61|1=1986}}, Tetsuo Kusumoto classified it as a unique weapon of the {{ill|Wakoku|en|Wakoku|qid=Q10888424|lt=Wa|1=Wakoku}} and began exploring its potential martial uses. However, due to the limited number of artifacts and the concentration of finds in southern Kyushu, the precise purpose of these swords remains unclear.<ref>Masafumi Itō, *Meandering Swords Unearthed in the Chūbu Region*.</ref>
== Notes ==
* As of {{ill|2008|en|2008|qid=Q2004|lt=Heisei 20|1=2008}}, around 70 examples of meandering swords had been found. Of these, 37 were excavated in Honshū, including 12 in the {{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}}, while the remaining examples were concentrated in southern Kyushu.<ref>Masafumi Itō, *Meandering Swords Unearthed in the Chūbu Region*.</ref>
* The largest known example, measuring 237 cm, was unearthed in {{ill|2023|en|2023|qid=Q49622|lt=Reiwa 5|1=2023}} from {{ill|Tomiomaru-yama Kofun|ja|富雄丸山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tomiomaru-yama Kofun|1=Tomio-Maruyama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28688548}} in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Japan's largest meandering sword and unique shield-shaped bronze mirror found at Tomiomaru-yama Kofun, Nara|url=https://www.sankei.com/article/20230125-B7RMG27LOJMBBECZOXTA63KAIM/|website=Sankei News|access-date=2023-02-25|language=ja|qq=}}</ref>
